fields.narrative_intro: *A wild-eyed person and a sweaty person discuss the Jacobian Conjecture in a parking garage during an alien invasion.*
fields.panel1:
[setup]
WILD-EYED PERSON: "Claude Fable just disproved the Jacobian Conjecture."
SWEATY PERSON: "The 87-year-old one?"
fields.panel2:
[build]
WILD-EYED PERSON: "Three points. One output. Jacobian still negative two."
SWEATY PERSON: "That's... that's impossible."
fields.panel3:
[pivot]
WILD-EYED PERSON: "Dixmier Conjecture probably falls too. As a corollary."
SWEATY PERSON: "As a COROLLARY?!"
fields.panel4:
[silent reaction]
[Visual: Both figures stand frozen mid-gesture. An alien invasion beam silently vaporizes the parking garage roof above them. Neither looks up.]
fields.panel5:
[punch]
SWEATY PERSON: "Has it been peer-reviewed?"
WILD-EYED PERSON: "The aliens landed six minutes ago."
fields.panel6:
[button]
[Visual: A passing alien examines a printout of F: ℂ³→ℂ³ and drops its ray gun, visibly unsettled.]

narrative_summary: Two people in a parking garage are so floored by Claude Fable's Jacobian Conjecture counterexample that an alien invasion happening simultaneously barely registers — until an alien reads the polynomial map and has its own existential crisis.
rationale: Candidate 2 earns its tie-breaking edge on punchline: 'The aliens landed six minutes ago' is a genuinely surprising non-sequitur answer to a straight question, recontextualizing the entire strip in one line rather than restating the setup. The alien dropping its ray gun at the polynomial map is a clean visual button that pays off the absurdist premise without over-explaining it.
story_title: Claude Fable produced a counterexample to the Jacobian Conjecture
place: in a parking garage during an alien invasion
hat_pair: foam cactus hat with pink plastic flowers on top
foam crown made of tangled ethernet cables
